topics.display_name: Advanced Sensor and Energy Harvesting Materials

source.host_organization_name: American Association for the Advancement of Science
host_organization_lineage_names: American Association for the Advancement of Science

crossref.publisher: American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS)
crossref.abstract:
<jats:p>
            Emerging wearable devices would benefit from integrating ductile photovoltaic light-harvesting power sources. In this work, we report a small-molecule acceptor (SMA), also known as a non–fullerene acceptor (NFA), designed for stretchable organic solar cell (
            <jats:italic>s</jats:italic>
            -OSC) blends with large mechanical compliance and performance. Blends of the organosilane-functionalized SMA BTP-Si4 with the polymer donor PNTB6-Cl achieved a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of >16% and ultimate strain (ε
            <jats:sub>u</jats:sub>
            ) of >95%. Typical SMAs suppress OSC blend ductility, but the addition of BTP-Si4 enhances it. Although BTP-Si4 is less crystalline than other SMAs, it retains considerable electron mobility and is highly miscible with PNTB6-Cl and is essential for enhancing ε
            <jats:sub>u</jats:sub>
            . Thus,
            <jats:italic>s</jats:italic>
            -OSCs with PCE > 14% and operating normally under various deformations (>80% PCE retention under an 80% strain) were demonstrated. Analysis of several SMA-polymer blends revealed general molecular structure–miscibility–stretchability relationships for designing ductile blends.
          </jats:p>
title: Mechanically robust and stretchable organic solar cells plasticized by small-molecule acceptors
